title: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium

items.subtopic: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors
items.title: Incidental Discovery of Primary Cardiac Tumors
items.content: Primary cardiac tumors are relatively uncommon, often discovered incidentally during imaging studies performed for other reasons.
keyPoints: Primary cardiac tumors are rare compared to secondary involvement in metastatic cancer (20% prevalence).
Many patients with primary cardiac tumors are asymptomatic at diagnosis.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors

items.subtopic: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors
items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Cardiac Tumors
items.content: Echocardiography is the initial imaging modality for evaluating cardiac tumors, but a multimodality approach may be necessary. Cardiac MRI can further differentiate lesions when echocardiography results are inconclusive.
keyPoints: Echocardiography is typically the first imaging test used to evaluate cardiac tumors.
Cardiac MRI helps in differentiating lesions based on size, location, myocardial infiltration, and gadolinium contrast perfusion.
Tissue histology remains essential for definitive diagnosis.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors

items.title: Prevalence and Nature of Primary Cardiac Tumors
items.content: Primary cardiac tumors are rare, with an incidence of ~1 in 2000 patients at autopsy. The majority (~75%) are benign myxomas, while malignant tumors account for about 25%. Early diagnosis is crucial due to the potential life-threatening complications and surgical curability.
keyPoints: Incidence: ~1 in 2000 patients
~75% of primary cardiac tumors are benign (mostly myxomas)
Malignant tumors account for ~25%
Early diagnosis is imperative due to potential life-threatening complications
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · PRIMARY TUMORS

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Cardiac Myxoma
items.content: Myxomas are the most common primary cardiac tumor in adults, often presenting with symptoms mimicking mitral valve disease. They can cause obstructive signs due to prolapse into the mitral orifice or valvular trauma. Echocardiography is key for diagnosis and surgical planning.
keyPoints: Myxomas account for one-third to one-half of primary cardiac tumors
Most common presentation mimics mitral valve disease (stenosis/regurgitation)
Echocardiography essential for diagnosis, assessing tumor size and attachment site
Symptoms may include fever, weight loss, arthralgias, and embolic phenomena
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · PRIMARY TUMORS

items.title: Genetic and Familial Aspects of Myxomas
items.content: Approximately 90% of myxomas are sporadic; the rest are familial, often part of Carney complex. Echocardiographic screening is recommended for first-degree relatives if the patient has multiple tumors or features of a myxoma syndrome.
keyPoints: ~90% of myxomas are sporadic
Familial myxomas may be part of Carney complex
Echocardiographic screening advised for first-degree relatives in familial cases
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · PRIMARY TUMORS

items.title: Surgical Indication and Recurrence Risk in Myxomas
items.content: Surgical excision using cardiopulmonary bypass is recommended for myxomas regardless of their size, as it is generally curative. The recurrence rate differs significantly between familial and sporadic cases.
keyPoints: Indicated surgical excision with cardiopulmonary bypass for all myxomas
Recurrence in 12–22% of familial cases
Recurrence in 1–2% of sporadic cases
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Myxoma | Malignant Tumors

items.title: Management and Clinical Presentation of Papillary Fibroelastomas
items.content: Papillary fibroelastomas are the most common cardiac valve tumors, often asymptomatic but can cause significant complications like embolization. Surgical resection is advised even for asymptomatic cases.
keyPoints: Most common tumors of cardiac valves
Can cause valve dysfunction and distal embolization
Resection recommended even if asymptomatic
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Myxoma | Malignant Tumors

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Rhabdomyomas in Children
items.content: Rhabdomyomas are the most common cardiac tumors in infants and children, often associated with tuberous sclerosis. They may regress spontaneously but require surgical intervention if obstructive.
keyPoints: Most common in infants/children; occur in ~50% of those with tuberous sclerosis
Associated with TSC1 and TSC2 mutations
Tend to regress, surgery needed for obstruction
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Myxoma | Malignant Tumors

items.subtopic: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors
items.question: Which imaging modality is usually the initial choice to evaluate cardiac tumors?
items.explanation: Echocardiography is typically the first imaging modality used to assess cardiac tumors.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors

items.subtopic: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors
items.question: What percentage of patients with end-stage metastatic cancer may have secondary involvement of the heart or pericardium?
items.explanation: Secondary cardiac involvement occurs in as many as 20% of patients with end-stage metastatic cancer.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors

items.subtopic: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors
items.question: What remains the gold standard for diagnosing cardiac tumors?
items.explanation: Tissue histology is the definitive method for diagnosing cardiac tumors.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors

items.question: What percentage of primary cardiac tumors are histologically benign?
items.explanation: Approximately three-quarters (75%) of primary cardiac tumors are histologically benign.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · PRIMARY TUMORS

items.question: Which type of tumor is most commonly associated with Carney complex?
items.explanation: Myxomas are the most common type of primary cardiac tumor in adults and are associated with Carney complex.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · PRIMARY TUMORS

items.question: What is a characteristic auscultatory finding in patients with myxoma?
items.explanation: A characteristic low-pitched sound, a 'tumor plop,' may be appreciated on auscultation during early or mid-diastole in patients with myxoma.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · PRIMARY TUMORS

items.question: Which imaging modality is useful for assessing tumor size and determining the site of attachment in cardiac myxomas?
items.explanation: Two-dimensional and three-dimensional transthoracic and/or transesophageal echocardiography are useful for assessing tumor size and determining the site of attachment in cardiac myxomas.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · PRIMARY TUMORS

items.question: What is the recurrence rate of myxomas in familial cases?
items.explanation: Myxomas recur in 12–22% of familial cases.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Myxoma | Malignant Tumors

items.question: Which cardiac tumor is most commonly associated with tuberous sclerosis?
items.explanation: Rhabdomyomas occur in ~50% of children with tuberous sclerosis.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Myxoma | Malignant Tumors

items.question: Which cardiac tumor is most likely to cause uncontrolled hypertension due to hormonal activity?
items.explanation: Paragangliomas are hormonally active and may result in uncontrolled hypertension.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Myxoma | Malignant Tumors

items.question: Which type of cardiac sarcoma is most common in adults?
items.explanation: Angiosarcomas are the most common type of cardiac sarcoma in adults.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Myxoma | Malignant Tumors

items.subtopic: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors
items.statement: Primary cardiac tumors are more common than secondary involvement of the heart.
items.explanation: Primary cardiac tumors, although uncommon, occur less frequently than secondary cardiac involvement.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors

items.subtopic: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors
items.statement: Cardiac MRI is never used in the evaluation of cardiac tumors.
items.explanation: Cardiac MRI can be useful when diagnosis remains uncertain after echocardiography.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors

items.statement: Malignant tumors account for approximately 75% of primary cardiac tumors.
items.explanation: Malignant tumors, almost all of which are sarcomas, account for only 25% of primary cardiac tumors.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · PRIMARY TUMORS

items.statement: Familial myxomas often occur as part of a syndrome complex.
items.explanation: The familial variety of myxomas often occurs as part of Carney complex, which includes multiple systemic features.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · PRIMARY TUMORS

items.statement: Fibromas are usually multiple and occur in children.
items.explanation: Fibromas are usually single, universally ventricular, and more common in infants and children.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Myxoma | Malignant Tumors

items.statement: Papillary fibroelastomas should be resected even when asymptomatic.
items.explanation: These tumors can cause valve dysfunction and embolization, so they are usually resected.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Myxoma | Malignant Tumors

items.assertion: Myxomas are the most common type of primary cardiac tumor in adults.
items.reason: They account for one-third to one-half of all cases at postmortem examination and approximately three-quarters of tumors treated surgically.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why myxomas are the most common type.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · PRIMARY TUMORS

items.assertion: Cardiac catheterization is mandatory before tumor resection.
items.reason: It provides essential information about coronary artery disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because cardiac catheterization is no longer considered mandatory when adequate noninvasive information is available.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · PRIMARY TUMORS

items.assertion: Rhabdomyomas have a tendency to regress completely or partially.
items.reason: They are associated with mutations in tumor-suppressor genes TSC1 and TSC2.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the regression is not directly explained by the genetic association.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Myxoma | Malignant Tumors

items.assertion: Sarcomas commonly involve the right side of the heart.
items.reason: They frequently invade the pericardial space and obstruct cardiac chambers or venae cavae.
items.explanation: Both are true, and the reason explains why sarcomas commonly involve the right side.
items.reference: Atrial Myxoma and Other Cardiac Tumors · Myxoma | Malignant Tumors
